-{"id":"bd-20fw","title":"Create WezTerm fork with wa feature flag and minimal integration points","description":"## Overview

Fork WezTerm and add the minimal code needed to support wa event delivery. All changes are behind \`#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]\`.

## Prerequisites

- Completed: bd-3gon (WaEventSink trait design)

## Repository Setup

### 1. Fork WezTerm
\`\`\`bash
gh repo fork wez/wezterm --clone
cd wezterm
git checkout -b feature/wa-integration
\`\`\`

### 2. Add Feature Flag
In Cargo.toml (workspace root):
\`\`\`toml
[workspace.features]
wa-integration = []
\`\`\`

In relevant crates (mux/Cargo.toml, wezterm/Cargo.toml):
\`\`\`toml
[features]
wa-integration = []
\`\`\`

## Modification Points

### 1. mux/src/lib.rs — Event Sink Registration

\`\`\`rust
#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
mod wa_events;

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
use wa_events::WaEventSink;

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
static WA_EVENT_SINK: OnceCell<Arc<dyn WaEventSink>> = OnceCell::new();

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
pub fn register_wa_event_sink(sink: Arc<dyn WaEventSink>) {
    WA_EVENT_SINK.set(sink).ok();
}

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
pub(crate) fn emit_wa_event(event: impl FnOnce(&dyn WaEventSink)) {
    if let Some(sink) = WA_EVENT_SINK.get() {
        event(sink.as_ref());
    }
}
\`\`\`

### 2. mux/src/pane.rs — Emit Output Events

In the method that receives PTY output:
\`\`\`rust
fn process_pty_output(&mut self, data: &[u8]) {
    // Existing WezTerm code...
    
    #[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
    crate::emit_wa_event(|sink| {
        sink.on_pane_output(self.pane_id, data);
    });
}
\`\`\`

### 3. mux/src/pane.rs — Emit State Changes

In methods that update pane state:
\`\`\`rust
fn set_title(&mut self, title: String) {
    // Existing code...
    
    #[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
    self.emit_state_change();
}

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
fn emit_state_change(&self) {
    crate::emit_wa_event(|sink| {
        sink.on_pane_state_change(self.pane_id, &self.get_wa_state());
    });
}
\`\`\`

### 4. mux/src/pane.rs — Emit User-Var Changes

In the OSC 1337 handler:
\`\`\`rust
fn set_user_var(&mut self, name: String, value: String) {
    // Existing code...
    
    #[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
    crate::emit_wa_event(|sink| {
        sink.on_user_var_changed(self.pane_id, &name, &value);
    });
}
\`\`\`

### 5. wezterm/src/main.rs — Initialize Sink

\`\`\`rust
#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
fn init_wa_integration() {
    if let Ok(socket_path) = std::env::var("WEZTERM_WA_SOCKET") {
        match wa_socket_sink::connect(&socket_path) {
            Ok(sink) => mux::register_wa_event_sink(sink),
            Err(e) => log::warn!("Failed to connect to wa socket: {}", e),
        }
    }
}
\`\`\`

## Testing the Fork

### 1. Build with Feature
\`\`\`bash
cargo build --features wa-integration
\`\`\`

### 2. Verify Default Build Unaffected
\`\`\`bash
cargo build  # Should work identically to upstream
\`\`\`

### 3. Integration Test
\`\`\`bash
# Start wa watch (which creates the socket)
wa watch &

# Start WezTerm with wa integration
WEZTERM_WA_SOCKET=/tmp/wa/events.sock ./target/debug/wezterm

# Verify events received
\`\`\`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] WezTerm fork created with feature/wa-integration branch
- [ ] Feature flag added to workspace
- [ ] Event emission added to pane output path
- [ ] Event emission added to state change paths
- [ ] Event emission added to user-var handler
- [ ] Default build (no feature) compiles and runs unchanged
- [ ] Feature build compiles and runs
- [ ] Events actually received by wa (integration test)

## Risks

- WezTerm internal APIs may change — minimize touchpoints
- Performance regression — benchmark before/after
- Thread safety bugs — careful with event emission

## Files to Modify (in WezTerm fork)

- Cargo.toml (workspace) — add feature
- mux/Cargo.toml — add feature
- mux/src/lib.rs — event sink registration
- mux/src/pane.rs — event emission
- wezterm/Cargo.toml — add feature
- wezterm/src/main.rs — initialization
- NEW: mux/src/wa_events.rs — trait + socket sink implementation

## References

- WezTerm mux: https://github.com/wez/wezterm/tree/main/mux

 {"id":"bd-21ml","title":"Benchmark WezTerm performance before/after STATUS_UPDATE_LUA removal","description":"## Overview

Measure WezTerm responsiveness before and after removing STATUS_UPDATE_LUA to quantify the performance improvement. This validates the optimization and provides data for the decision to pursue (or skip) Phase 2/3.

## Methodology

### 1. Baseline Measurement (Before)

With STATUS_UPDATE_LUA active:

\`\`\`bash
# Terminal responsiveness test
time for i in {1..1000}; do echo "line $i"; done

# Scrollback stress test
seq 1 10000 | while read n; do echo "Stress test line $n with some padding text to fill the line"; done

# Interactive typing latency (subjective)
# Type rapidly and observe lag
\`\`\`

### 2. Measurement (After)

Same tests with STATUS_UPDATE_LUA removed.

### 3. Metrics to Capture

| Metric | How to Measure |
|--------|----------------|
| Echo throughput | Lines/second in echo loop |
| Scroll smoothness | Subjective + frame timing |
| Input latency | Time from keypress to echo (harder to measure) |
| CPU usage | \`htop\` or \`perf\` during tests |
| WezTerm frame rate | If profiling tools available |

### 4. Test Scenarios

1. **Idle pane**: Just cursor blinking — update-status fires constantly
2. **Active output**: \`yes\` or \`seq\` flooding terminal
3. **Multiple panes**: 4+ panes, some active
4. **With wa watch running**: Baseline vs optimized
5. **Without wa watch**: Pure WezTerm baseline

## Tools

### CPU Profiling
\`\`\`bash
# Linux perf
perf record -g -p $(pgrep wezterm) -- sleep 10
perf report

# macOS Instruments
instruments -t "Time Profiler" -p $(pgrep wezterm) -D profile.trace
\`\`\`

### Frame Timing
WezTerm may have debug options for frame timing. Check config:
\`\`\`lua
config.debug_key_events = true
-- Other debug options
\`\`\`

### Memory
\`\`\`bash
# Watch WezTerm memory usage
while true; do ps -o rss= -p $(pgrep wezterm); sleep 1; done
\`\`\`

## Expected Results

| Scenario | Before (Lua) | After (No Lua) | Improvement |
|----------|--------------|----------------|-------------|
| Idle CPU | ~5-15% | ~1-3% | 3-5x |
| Echo throughput | X lines/s | Y lines/s | TBD |
| Typing latency | Perceptible lag | No lag | Subjective |

## Recording Results

Document results in:
1. This bead's comments (use \`br comments add\`)
2. PR description when merging
3. CHANGELOG.md under "Performance" section

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Baseline measurements recorded (before)
- [ ] Post-optimization measurements recorded (after)
- [ ] Improvement quantified (percentage or ratio)
- [ ] Results documented
- [ ] User confirms subjective improvement
- [ ] Decision: proceed to Phase 2/3 or not?

## Dependencies

- Should be done: After bd-30gj (STATUS_UPDATE_LUA removed)
- Before: bd-mwst (documentation) — to include benchmark results

## Files

- Create: benchmarks/lua_removal_results.md (or add to docs/)

## Notes

This is a **validation task**. If results don't show improvement, we need to investigate why (maybe the bottleneck is elsewhere).","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:52:05.693319255Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T23:09:13.713378695Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T23:09:13.713234457Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-21ml","depends_on_id":"bd-2l5s","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:52:05.709998681Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-21ml","depends_on_id":"bd-30gj","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:52:15.720326696Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":76,"issue_id":"bd-21ml","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Benchmark Status

**Note**: Baseline measurements (before STATUS_UPDATE_LUA removal) were not captured before the optimization was implemented. The code was removed as part of bd-30gj and bd-2z6d.

### Theoretical Improvement

The `update-status` Lua event was firing at ~60Hz (every frame), which caused:
- Continuous Lua interpreter invocations
- Repeated JSON serialization of pane state
- IPC overhead for each status update

With STATUS_UPDATE_LUA removed:
- Alt-screen detection now uses escape sequence parsing (zero Lua overhead)
- Pane metadata obtained from `wezterm cli list` only when needed
- No continuous polling or event firing

### Recommendation

Close this task and document the change in bd-mwst. User can validate subjective improvement by testing WezTerm responsiveness with/without `wa watch` running.

### Files Changed (for reference)
- Removed STATUS_UPDATE_LUA from setup.rs
- Removed StatusUpdate types/handlers from ipc.rs  
- Removed StatusUpdateReceived event from events.rs
- Created screen_state.rs for escape sequence-based alt-screen detection

 {"id":"bd-3kzu","title":"TUI/Web surfaces for saved searches","description":"## What
Expose saved searches in TUI and web dashboards.

## Why
Operators should be able to view/trigger saved searches without memorizing CLI flags.

## How
- TUI: list saved searches, run on demand, toggle enabled
- Web: read-only list + last run status (no mutations yet)

## Risks
- Feature gating: keep behind tui/web feature flags

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.4","title":"[EPIC] Diagnostic Bundle + Crash Capture","description":"# [EPIC] Diagnostic Bundle + Crash Capture

## Mission
Make it easy to answer "Is wa healthy?" and "Why did it do that?" without guesswork, including crash-only diagnostics.

## Deliverables
- `wa doctor` checks (environment + config + DB)
- health snapshot plumbing from the watcher
- diagnostic bundle export (sanitized)
- crash bundle capture + listing/triage integration
- performance budget checks in CI

## Deferred
- Incident/reproduce workflows are deferred until demand (see wa-upg.1).

## Testing & observability (non-negotiable)
Diagnostics tooling is only valuable if it is reliable and safe.

- Unit/integration tests must verify:
  - doctor checks against fixtures (no real WezTerm required)
  - diagnostic/crash bundle contents and layout
  - redaction: bundles/logs never leak secrets

- E2E scripts must verify:
  - `wa doctor` behaves correctly in healthy vs broken scenarios
  - crashes produce actionable messages and point to artifacts

## Success Criteria
- `wa doctor`/`wa status` provide actionable health signals (DB connectivity, queue depths, ingest lag, lock state).
- Diagnostic/crash bundles include logs, effective config, and redacted diagnostics for bug reports.
- Performance budgets exist for core hot paths and regressions are visible in CI.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.4.1","title":"[Human command] `wa doctor` (environment + config + DB checks)","description":"# Task: Human command wa doctor

## Goal
Provide a deterministic checklist that validates the wa environment.

Doctor is a user trust surface: it must make failures actionable and help users understand where wa stores state.

## Checks (initial)
- Workspace/config resolution
  - print resolved workspace root
  - print resolved DB/log/lock/socket paths
  - verify workspace is writable

- WezTerm
  - wezterm CLI available (wezterm cli list works)
  - wezterm version meets minimum supported
  - scrollback settings are sufficient (warn if too low; best-effort)

- Storage
  - DB is readable/writable
  - schema/migration version matches
  - WAL + foreign_keys configured

- Watcher status (if running)
  - last tick time
  - tailer count
  - backlog sizes

- Feature availability
  - browser (playwright)
  - mcp
  - web/tui/sync (optional)

## Output
- Rich panels when TTY.
- Non-TTY JSON suitable for automation.

## Safety
- Output must never include secrets (even if config contains tokens).
- Redaction must be applied to any potentially sensitive field.

## Testing
- Unit/integration tests: `wa-nu4.3.4.8` (fixture-based checks + output stability)
- E2E: `wa-4vx.10.22` (healthy/broken scenarios with verbose artifacts)

## Acceptance Criteria
- On a healthy system, wa doctor exits 0 with a concise summary.
- On a broken system, it exits non-zero and lists actionable fixes.
- Output never includes secrets (even if config contains tokens).

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.4.3","title":"Diagnostic bundle export (sanitized): logs/config/DB stats/recent events","description":"# Task: Diagnostic bundle export (sanitized)

## Goal
Generate a single artifact users can attach to bug reports that contains:
- environment info (OS, wa version)
- config summary (redacted)
- DB health stats (counts, schema version, WAL size)
- last N events + workflow step logs (redacted)
- **active pane reservations + recent reservation conflicts (redacted)**

## Safety requirements
- Never include secrets.
- Apply PolicyEngine redaction rules to any text fields.
- Prefer summaries over raw transcripts.

## Output
- A directory or archive (e.g. `.tar.zst`) written to a user-specified path.

## Testing
- Unit/integration tests: `wa-nu4.3.4.7` validates:
  - redaction (no secrets)
  - stable file layout
  - stable metadata fields
  - works while watcher is running

## Acceptance Criteria
- Bundle can be generated while watcher is running.
- Bundle contents are stable and documented.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.4.4","title":"Perf budgets + regression checks (ingest tick, pattern match, FTS inserts)","description":"# Task: Perf budgets + regression checks

## Goal
Define and enforce basic performance budgets so wa stays fast as features accrete.

## Key hot paths
- Quick reject / prefiltering (if applicable)
- Delta extraction + seq assignment
- Pattern detection over deltas
- DB writes (segments + FTS)

## Deliverables
- Criterion benches for hot paths (some may already exist; extend to cover end-to-end ingest tick).
- Budget thresholds (warn or fail) suitable for CI.

## Testing / CI expectations
- Use coarse budgets to avoid noisy failures.
- CI should detect gross regressions (order-of-magnitude) reliably and upload benchmark artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- CI can detect a gross regression (e.g., 10× slowdown) reliably.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.4.5","title":"Metrics endpoint (Prometheus): wa watch --metrics bind + core counters/histograms","description":"# Task: Metrics endpoint (Prometheus)

## Goal
Expose optional Prometheus metrics to support long-running reliability:
- ingest lag
- gaps
- queue depths
- pattern detection latency
- workflow outcomes
- DB write latencies

## Design
- Disabled by default.
- Bind address configurable (default localhost).
- Must not significantly slow down hot path.

## Safety
- Default bind is localhost-only.
- Binding publicly requires an explicit, scary flag (proposed): `--dangerous-bind-any`.
- Metrics must never include transcript contents or secrets.

## Deliverables
- `wa watch --metrics <bind>` and/or config-driven enable.
- A minimal set of stable metric names.

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - metric names are stable
  - registry can be instantiated with metrics disabled (zero overhead path)

- Integration tests:
  - start watcher with metrics enabled (bind to localhost ephemeral port)
  - fetch `/metrics` and assert:
    - response is valid Prometheus text format
    - key metrics appear (even if counts are zero)

- Safety tests:
  - default bind is localhost
  - public bind requires `--dangerous-bind-any`
  - enabling metrics does not leak secrets

## Acceptance Criteria
- When enabled, `/metrics` responds with valid Prometheus text format.
- When disabled, zero overhead on hot path.

 {"id":"wa-upg.12","title":"[EPIC] Backpressure-aware watcher (bounded queues + graceful degradation)","description":"# [EPIC] Backpressure-aware watcher

## Mission
Ensure the watcher remains stable under load by:
- enforcing bounded queues everywhere
- adapting polling/work under backpressure
- degrading safely (explicit GAP) rather than deadlocking or OOMing

## Why This Matters
Backpressure problems are the #1 way background daemons become unreliable:
- memory grows
- latency spikes
- the system "hangs" and users lose trust

## Scope
- Define backpressure signals and thresholds.
- Implement adaptive behavior:
  - slow down polling when queues are deep
  - batch work
  - emit health warnings
  - record GAP events when continuity can't be guaranteed
- Ensure health output makes backpressure obvious.

## Success Criteria
- Under sustained load, watcher stays responsive and memory-bounded.
- Backpressure behavior is visible and explainable.

## Testing
- Integration tests with slow consumers.
- E2E stress scenario that triggers backpressure and verifies graceful behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Watcher has explicit backpressure handling and no unbounded buffers.
- Tests and E2E validate behavior and produce good artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-upg.12.2","title":"System-wide backpressure propagation + instrumentation (no unbounded queues)","description":"# Task: System-wide backpressure propagation + instrumentation

## Goal
Ensure backpressure is handled **end-to-end** across the watcher, not just in one component.

Base backpressure behaviors are already part of the ingest/storage foundations (e.g., tailers + event bus + storage writer queues).
This task hardens the *system-level* behavior:
- propagate backpressure signals across subsystems
- prevent deadlocks/starvation
- make backpressure visible to operators and test harnesses

## Requirements
- Define a small set of standard backpressure signals (examples):
  - ingest queue depth / lag
  - storage writer queue depth / lag
  - pattern engine backlog / compute time budget
  - notification queue depth (if any)
- Propagate signals to:
  - polling scheduler (slow down responsibly)
  - batching decisions (increase batch size, reduce wakeups)
  - health/status surfaces (warnings + actionable guidance)
- Starvation avoidance:
  - ensure one noisy pane cannot starve others
  - ensure slow consumer paths do not deadlock the system

## Testing
- Integration tests:
  - synthetic slow consumers
  - multiple panes with one "noisy" producer
  - verify bounded memory + fairness

## Acceptance Criteria
- Under backpressure, the system stays responsive and memory-bounded.
- Backpressure transitions are visible in logs and health output.
- No unbounded buffers remain in the watcher hot path.

 {"id":"wa-upg.12.3","title":"Overflow strategy: explicit GAP insertion and safe degradation","description":"# Task: Overflow strategy (explicit GAP)

## Goal
When continuity cannot be maintained due to backpressure, fail safely and explicitly.

## Requirements
- Define when to emit GAP due to overload:
  - queue overflow
  - sustained lag beyond threshold
- Ensure GAP handling:
  - is visible in health/triage
  - causes workflows to pause rather than act on uncertain state
- Record evidence:
  - what overflowed and why

## Testing
- Integration tests simulate queue overflow and assert GAP recording.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The system never silently drops data; it emits explicit uncertainty.
